ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for fried mock duck; mapped to wheat gluten food product as the closest defensible ancestor. Fried mock duck is a deep-fried wheat gluten and soybean flour product shaped and seasoned to resemble duck.
ontology.foodex2_description: Wheat gluten (seitan) — processed plant-based meat analogue
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (wheat gluten) used as closest defensible anchor. No specific FoodEx2 entry exists for fried mock duck as a prepared dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for fried mock duck or wheat gluten meat analogue in the clinical terminology system.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fried mock duck is approximately 100 g, equivalent to three to four pieces, as commonly served as a side dish or protein component in a Singaporean meal.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Mock Duck — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Fried Mock Duck (cr_id F06020001). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fried mock duck. GI estimated at 54 (low) based on the closest analogous food: wheat gluten (seitan) products, which have a low GI due to very high protein content (20.5 g/100g) slowing gastric emptying and glucose absorption. Glycemic load per 100 g serving = GI × carbohydrate_g / 100 = 54 × 9.5 / 100 = 5.1 (low). Source: University of Sydney GI Database analogous wheat gluten entry and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 827 mg per 100 g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension management
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit fried mock duck due to its very high sodium content of 827 mg/100 g, driven by soy sauce and vegetarian oyster sauce in the marinade; if consumed, restrict to a small portion of 30–40 g and request reduced-sodium preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (9.5 g/100 g, sugar 0 g) and very high protein (20.5 g/100 g) support glycaemic stability, but high sodium warrants caution
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fried mock duck has a low estimated GI (~54) and low carbohydrate load (GL ~5.1 per 100 g), making it a reasonable protein source for blood glucose management in type 2 diabetes; however, the very high sodium content (827 mg/100 g) increases cardiovascular risk, which is already elevated in this population, so portion control and reduced-sodium preparation are strongly advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (827 mg/100 g) and deep-fried preparation generating advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) both elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid fried mock duck due to its high sodium content (827 mg/100 g) and deep-fried preparation, which generates AGEs that promote vascular inflammation and endothelial dysfunction; a steamed or braised low-sodium alternative is strongly preferred.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 827 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg RA threshold; deep-fried preparation generates AGEs that worsen joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Fried mock duck is unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to sodium at 827 mg/100 g (far exceeding the 400 mg RA threshold) and deep-frying generating AGEs that exacerbate synovial inflammation; request steamed or braised mock duck with reduced soy sauce as a safer alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried mock duck is a Singaporean Chinese vegetarian dish made from deep-fried mashed wheat gluten (seitan) and soybean flour, shaped and seasoned to resemble duck meat. The product is marinated in soy sauce, vegetarian oyster sauce, sesame oil, and garlic before deep-frying, giving it a golden-brown, crispy exterior with a dense, chewy, meat-like interior. It is a popular plant-based protein source in Singapore's vegetarian and Buddhist cuisine, commonly served as a side dish in economic rice (cai png) stalls or part of a mixed vegetarian meal. Nutritionally, fried mock duck is high in protein (20.5 g/100 g) and moderate in fat (10.1 g/100 g) from the deep-frying process, with low carbohydrate (9.5 g/100 g) and zero sugar. Its estimated glycemic index is low (~54), making it suitable for blood glucose management. However, its sodium content is very high at 827 mg/100 g, driven by the soy-based marinade, making it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis without significant portion restriction or recipe modification. It contains gluten and soy allergens and is suitable for vegetarians and vegans. The deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which are a concern for inflammatory conditions.
common_user_queries: What is mock duck made of?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06020001) with confidence 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary approved regional source for Singaporean foods. GI is an analogous estimate based on wheat gluten products from the University of Sydney GI Database, appropriately documented.
notes: sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database entry; this is consistent with the unsweetened soy-based marinade formulation. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for fried mock duck are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F06020001 as of 2026. GI is an analogous estimate (~54, low) based on wheat gluten (seitan) products from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for fried mock duck was found in approved sources. Halal status is not confirmed as the product may contain non-halal certified soy sauce or oyster sauce depending on the manufacturer.
